name: VibecodeApp – OpenCode-Enhanced Context Cards, MCP Server, and TUI Dashboard
description: Add context cards, separated facts/heuristics, an MCP server for OpenCode and an interactive TUI dashboard. Each task is designed for ~15 minutes of focused work, followed by an explicit check-and-fix step to ensure quality and integration with OpenCode.

tasks.title: Implement context card generation and fact/heuristic separation
tasks.description: Extend `src/vibecodeapp/` with new modules: `schema.py` (dataclasses ContextCard, RiskItem, Fact, Heuristic), `ast_parser.py` (extract module docstring and function/class symbols via Python's `ast`), and `risk_analyzer.py` (scan files for deterministic facts like TODO/FIXME and unsafe permissions, plus heuristics such as high parameter count or suspicious names). Modify `inventory.py` to accept `generate_cards=True`, call the parser for every Python file, build ContextCard objects, and output them in `.vibecode/file_inventory.json` alongside the plain file list. Modify the risk reporter to store separate `facts` and `heuristics` arrays in `.vibecode/risk_report.json`. Ensure both JSON files are written by the `vibecode inventory` command. Add a config file `.vibecode/config.yml` to set card detail level (basic/full) and whether heuristics are computed, with sensible defaults. This task covers the full implementation; aim to produce testable, importable code without yet running end‑to‑end checks.

tasks.title: Check & fix: verify context cards and risk report, correct imperfections
tasks.description: Run `vibecode inventory` on the project itself. Check that `.vibecode/file_inventory.json` contains a `context_cards` key with valid cards for all Python files. Each card must have a non-empty `purpose` (the docstring), a list of symbols with `name`, `kind`, and `line`, and the `content_snippet` of the first 200 characters. Verify that `.vibecode/risk_report.json` contains separated `facts` and `heuristics` lists, and that the heuristic items include severity levels. Spot‑check a few files manually. If any field is missing or incorrectly formatted, fix the extraction logic. Adjust the config loader to handle missing files gracefully. Correct any import errors. Document any remaining quirks. This step ensures the foundation for MCP and TUI is rock‑solid.

tasks.title: Implement MCP server with OpenCode‑focused tools
tasks.description: Create `src/vibecodeapp/mcp_server.py` using the `mcp` package. Define a `VibecodeServer` class that reads `file_inventory.json` and `risk_report.json` at startup. Register three tools: `get_file_card(file_path: str)` – returns a human‑readable card with purpose, symbols, snippet, facts and heuristics; `find_symbol(symbol_name: str)` – returns a JSON array of file paths and symbol details; `list_high_risk()` – returns high‑severity heuristics from the risk report. Add a CLI command `vibecode serve` that starts the server with stdio transport and prints a ready‑to‑paste OpenCode MCP configuration snippet to stderr. Handle edge cases (missing inventory, unknown symbol) with clear error messages. This task focuses purely on writing the server code; do not yet test it.

tasks.title: Check & fix: test MCP server with Inspector, refine tool behavior
tasks.description: Use `npx @modelcontextprotocol/inspector` to connect to `vibecode serve`. Test each tool with real project data: call `get_file_card` for an existing file and a non‑existent one (expect error), `find_symbol` for a function that appears in multiple files, and `list_high_risk` after generating a risk report with some high‑severity heuristics. Verify that all responses are well‑structured and informative. If the inspector reveals missing data or crashes, fix the server logic. Adjust the tool output format to be more OpenCode‑friendly (plain text with markdown). Ensure the configuration snippet printed on startup is valid and contains the correct command. This step closes the MCP loop and confirms it works with OpenCode's expected protocol.

tasks.title: Build TUI dashboard with card table, stats footer and detail screen
tasks.description: Add `textual` as a dependency. Create `src/vibecodeapp/tui_app.py` containing a `VibecodeTUI` app. Implement the main screen with a `DataTable` populated from the loaded context cards (columns: File, Purpose, Symbols). Add a `Footer` widget that shows total file count, card count, and high‑risk item count (read from `risk_report.json`). Bind the Enter key to push a new screen with the full card detail: purpose, a table of symbols, lists of facts and heuristics, and a code snippet block. Style the interface with an external `tui_theme.tcss` file using a dark, CLI‑friendly palette. Wire this into the CLI as `vibecode dashboard`, reusing the same data loader function (to be created later, for now inline loading is acceptable). This task produces a self‑contained navigable dashboard ready for review.

tasks.title: Check & fix: run dashboard, verify navigation and data integrity
tasks.description: Launch `vibecode dashboard` and inspect the table. Check that all Python files appear with correct purpose and a representative symbol list. Press Enter on several rows to confirm the detail screen loads and displays all card fields. Test the back button. Ensure the footer counters match the actual number of cards and high‑risk items. If any card is missing a field that causes the detail screen to crash, fix the data loader to provide safe defaults (e.g., empty lists instead of None). Validate the theme applies correctly. This step guarantees the TUI is a reliable visual companion to the MCP server.

tasks.title: Unify data loading and connect TUI + MCP + CLI
tasks.description: Create `src/vibecodeapp/data_loader.py` with a single function `load_project_data()` that reads both `file_inventory.json` and `risk_report.json` and returns a `ProjectData` dataclass. Replace the duplicate loading logic in the MCP server and TUI app with calls to this function. Update the `vibecode serve` and `vibecode dashboard` commands to gracefully handle missing data (prompt to run `vibecode inventory`). Add a simple integration test script (`tests/test_integration.py`) that generates inventory, starts the dashboard in headless mode (if possible) and probes the MCP server via a subprocess. This task consolidates the architecture and ensures a single source of truth.

tasks.title: Check & fix: run full integration, final polish and documentation
tasks.description: Execute the end‑to‑end workflow: `vibecode inventory` → `vibecode dashboard` (quick visual check) → `vibecode serve` and connect from OpenCode. Use OpenCode to call `get_file_card` on a real project file, `find_symbol` on a known class, and `list_high_risk`. Capture any errors or poor formatting and fix them immediately. Enhance help texts in the CLI (e.g., `vibecode serve --help`). Update README.md with clear instructions for setting up OpenCode, launching the dashboard, and interpreting risk reports. Ensure the entire pipeline feels cohesive. This final check ensures the project is ready for daily use with OpenCode as the primary agent.
